ankle: What region is pain present in?
When do you feel the pain?
Do you experience ankle joint stiffness?
Do you feel the pain about 4 cm above your heel?
What type of pain do you feel?
Sharp and pinching		(Rule out tarsal tunnel syndrome)
Is there evidence of inflammation around the heel?
Do you experience pain during push-off phase of walking?
Does the pain reduce with rest at joint or with the use of NSAIDs?
Do you feel the pain about 5cm from your heel?
Do you engage in sports requiring heavy push-offs such as: squash, badminton, football, tennis, netball?
Have you had a previous history of tendinitis?
Did you hear a ripping or popping sensation accompanying the pain at onset?
Did you feel like someone hit you on the heel at the onset of the pain?
Have you self-medicated with local corticosteroids before?
Check if plantarflexion of the ankle is weak.
Check for palpable gap at the site of the rupture
Thompson’s or Simmond’s Test: Patient lies prone and the calf is squeezed. If tendon is ruptured, the foot will remain still when it ought to contract involuntarily.
Radiographic Imaging: Use ultrasound to confirm the diagnosis.
Is pain and tenderness localised to the insertion of the Tendon Achilles?
Check for density of apophysitis and check for fragmentation of the apophysitis.
Female		(NB: Can occur in younger females)
Where do you feel the pain?
Do you have painful bumps on your heel?
Do you experience this more during cold weather times?
Does the posterior portion of the calcaneum look more prominent?
Do you experience pain on heel or sole with standing and/or walking?
Is this pain most prominent at the heel?
Is the onset of pain sudden or gradual?
Sudden 		(Ask if patient has just recently changed their footwear)
Is pain worse when you first get up in the morning?
Yes	(Though this is not in every case)
Do you feel the pain more when you get up after you have been sitting for a long time?
Is there tenderness at the medial aspect beneath the heel / mid-foot?
Confirmation using ultrasound to check for thickening around the heel.
Doppler test to show increased local blood flow and neovascularisation
Did you stumble on a stairway, potholes or pavement before the onset of pain?
Have you experienced any twisting or wrenching movement at your ankle joint?
Can you walk with the affected foot and bear weight on it?
Is bruise at sight of injury faint and did it appear slowly?
For Acute Injury of Lateral Ligaments
Is severe pain felt on slight attempt to invert (turn inward) foot?
Palpate the ankle, distally and anteriorly to the lateral malleolus and ask if patient feels more pain. Usually, patient feels more pain on palpation.
Rule out undisplaced fracture of Fibula, tarsal bone, 5th metatarsal bone
Have you had a past episode of an ankle sprain?
If yes, have you had experiences like your ankle giving way OR instability when walking on uneven surfaces?
No		(proceed to special test either ways)
Special Test for Recurrent Lateral Instability

Talar tilt test: The ankle is held in the neutral position, examiner stabilised the tibia just above the ankle and then tries to force the ankle into maximum inversion. The ROM can be measured or compared with the contralateral side.
Inversion laxity suggests injury to calcaneofibular and anterior talofibular ligaments (ATFL).

Anterior Drawer Test: Patient sits with knee flexed at 90 deg and ankle in plantarflexion of about 10 deg. The lower leg is stabilised by the examiner and the force patient’s heel forward over the tibia. If positive, the talus can be felt sliding forwards and backwards. Laxity indicates tear of the ATFL.
Do you have a previous history of fibular fracture?
Check if foot is everted and externally rotated.
Check for widening of the medial joint space in the mortise view, check if talus is tilted, check for fracture or dislocation of the proximal fibula.
For Tears of Inferior Tibiofibular Ligaments:
Did you have an injury that twisted your ankle?
Do you feel pains in front of your ankle?
Is swelling and marked tenderness present directly over the inferior tibiofibular joint?
Squeeze Test: Compress the leg firmly above the ankle. Test is positive if pain is felt over the syndesmosis.
FOR MALLEOLAR FRACTURES OF THE ANKLE
Any history of fall prior to the onset of the injury?
Did you experience any twisting injury with your ankle going into inversion?
Did you experience a fall with your foot anchored to the ground while your body fell forward?
Is any obvious deformity present in the ankle?
Following the onset of the pain are you able to stand and bear weight on the affected part?
Did you experience a fall from a height?
Were you involved in a road traffic accident?
Is your foot painful and swollen?
Does the heel look broad and wide
Is the surrounding tissue around the heel thick and tender?
Is the normal concavity (hollow) below the lateral malleolus lacking?
Can you invert (turn inward) and evert (turn outward) your foot?
No 		Rule out foot compartment syndrome.
Do you feel intense pain in the foot?
Is sensation diminished at the foot?
Do you experience pain on the dorsiflexion of the toes?
Check if two-point discrimination is present.
Check if pain is present on passive movement of the toes
Do you feel uncomfortable when you wear your shoes?
Since the onset of this discomfort, do you walk less due to the discomfort?
Do you feel pain at your metatarsophalangeal joint (forefoot)?
Is the region of the metatarsophalangeal joint (forefoot) swollen?
Is there tenderness at the metatarsaophalangeal joint (forefoot)?
Do you feel pain when you press or squeeze the forefoot?
Did this pain progress to the entire foot?
Do you experience weakness of your foot muscles?
Prominence of the metatarsal head in the soles (Feels like walking on stones)
Check for osteoporosis, and periarticular erosion at the metatarsophalangeal joint.
For RA of Ankle and Hindfoot
Do you find it increasingly difficult to walk?
Do you experience pain around your ankle?
Is swelling and tenderness present behind the lateral and/or medial malleolus?
Do you find it painful to invert (turn inward) and evert (turn outward) your foot?
Do you find the range of movement limited when you try to invert (turn inward) and evert (turn outward) your foot??
Is a valgus deformity (turning outward of the foot) present?
Radiographic confirmation. Check for osteoporosis, erosion of the tarsal and ankle joints and the swelling of soft-tissues.
Have you had a previous diagnosis of osteoarthritis of other joints?
Do you have any previous history of the following: malunited fracture, recurrent instability, and avascular necrosis of the talus?
Do you experience pain and stiffness at the ankle joint? *
Do you notice this pain more when standing up from rest?
Do you experience limitation when bringing the foot upward towards (dorsiflexion) and pushing the foot downward away (plantar flexion) from the shin?
are palpable anterior osteophytes present at the ankle joint
is tenderness present along the anterior joint line.
is outward turning of the foot as patient walks?
Radiographic confirmation – check for joint space narrowing, osteophytes formation, subchondral sclerosis, and cyst formation.
Do you feel numbness, burning and tingling sensation in your foot and/or medial portion of your ankle?
Do you experience weakness in your foot muscles (toe abductors and flexors)?
Do you have a previous history of diabetes or osteoarthritis?
Does your job or daily task involve running, excessive walking and standing?
Do you have a previous history of trauma or dislocation at ankle region?
Is tenderness present behind the medial malleolus?
Do the symptoms you feel worsen with a forceful eversion (turning outward) of the foot OR dorsiflexion (moving foot upwards towards the shin)?
Do you feel a stabbing pain in the medial sole of your foot while walking?
Does the pain get aggravated with walking?
Do you feel night pain that wake you up from sleep?
Check for atrophy of the abductor hallucis
Check for the position of the talus and calcaneus
Tinel’s sign: Percussion of the tarsal tunnel. Positive if this results in a distal radiation of paraesthesia
Dorsiflexion-Eversion Test: Place the patient’s foot into dorsiflexion and eversion and hold for 5-10 seconds. Positive if patient’s symptom is elicited.
cervical: Do you feel numbness in any form?
Which of these movements currently aggravates your pain?
Do you feel more pain when you move your neck?
Do you experience some stiffness in your neck?
Does the pain increase with coughing, sneezing, straining or laughing?
Do you have any restrictions with movement around your neck?
Do you also have any pain around your lower back, buttocks, thigh or legs?
Does the pain wake you up at night?
Do you feel any form of muscle weakness?
Do you experience impaired sensations in your arm and forearm?
Check if cervical rotation on the affected side is less than 60 degrees.

Spurling’s Test (Maximal Cervical Compression and Foraminal Compression Test): The neck is placed in extension, lateral flexion, and axial compression. The test is positive if pain is present and radiates to the arm.

Cervical Distraction Test: The patient lies supine and the neck is positioned comfortably.  The examiner places hand around the patient’s mastoid process. The examiner then slightly flexes the patient’s neck and pull the head towards your torso.
Test is positive if the symptoms reduces or is eliminated with traction.
Do you feel numbness in any form?
Which of these movements currently aggravates your pain?
Do you feel more pain when you move your neck?
Does the pain relieve you with rest?
Does the pain increase with coughing, sneezing, straining, or laughing?
Do you experience intermittent neck pain and shoulder pain?
Do you have any restrictions with movement around your neck?
Do you also have any pain around your lower back, buttocks, thigh, or legs?
Does the pain wake you up at night?
Do you feel any form of muscle weakness?

Spurling’s Test (Maximal Cervical Compression and Foraminal Compression Test): The neck is placed in extension, lateral flexion, and axial compression. The test is positive if pain is present and radiates to the arm.
Radiographic confirmation: check for loss of normal cervical lordosis, and degeneration.
elbow: What region is pain present in?
Do you have a recent history of fall or trauma in the affected region?
Was the pain onset sudden or gradual?
Sudden (Rule out rupture, link to avulsion of the biceps tendon)
Do you experience tingling or abnormal sensations?
Do the following: lifting an object, repetitive wrist extension, gripping objects tightly, and twisting/rotating the forearm as in using a screwdriver or opening a door knob aggravate this pain?
NB: If yes to 6, probe the following conditions.
LATERAL ELBOW TENDINOPATHY (aka Tennis Elbow or Lateral Epicondylitis)
Does your job or daily tasks involve repetitive forearm movement?
If yes, describe the level of your repetitive activities involving the forearm prior to this condition.
Do your symptoms radiate to the forearm?
Do you experience early morning stiffness?
Do you experience weakness in the muscles of the forearm?
Do you experience discomfort during activities involving lifting, shaking of hands, and gripping?
Is palpation of the lateral epicondyle painful?
Maudsley’s test: Resisted extension of the middle finger elicits pain
Cozen’s test: Pain on resisted wrist extension
Mill’s Sign: Pt closes hands, dorsiflexes wrist and elbow is extended. The examiner then forces the wrist into palmar flexion against resistance; pain in the epicondyle is positive.
Is tenderness present over the medial epicondyle?
Does your job or daily tasks involve repetitive forearm movement?
If yes, describe the level of your repetitive activities involving the forearm prior to this condition.
Do your symptoms radiate to the forearm?
Do you experience early morning stiffness?
Do you experience weakness in the muscles of the forearm?
Do you experience discomfort during activities involving lifting and shaking of hands?
The patient resists forearm pronation with the forearm in extension.
Patient resists wrist flexion with the forearm in extension and supination (i.e. clinician resists wrist extension with the forearm in extension and supination)
The test is positive if pain is elicited.
Rule out ulnar nerve entrapment and medial ligament injury
Do you experience pain, numbness or abnormal sensation in the forearm, fourth and ring finger?
Do you experience weakness of the hand muscles?
Check if muscle bulk in hand is normal (Extreme cases may include atrophy)

Valgus Stress Test: Place forearm in supine position, flex elbow up till about 30 deg. Palpate MCL and apply lateral pressure (Abduction)
Positive test: Increased laxity of MCL and pain.
Do you exercise often by lifting weights?
Check if pain is present on resisted forearm supination?
Pain indicates the presence of a biceps tendinopathy
AVULSION OF THE DISTAL BICEPS TENDON
Did you perform any strenuous activity recently involving your forearm?
Do you feel weakness in the anterior portion of your forearm?
Did you feel a tearing sensation accompanying the pain onset?
Did you observe a shift in the position of the biceps belly?
O’Driscoll’s hook test: Palpate for biceps tendon in the antecubital fossa with arms flexed, shoulder ABducted and forearm in supination.
Test is positive if the biceps tendon cannot be hooked
History of repeated pressure or friction to the olecranon?
If yes to 1 and 2, rule out rheumatoid arthritis.
Is a nodule present at the back of the elbow?
Do you have episodes of fever that resolve spontaneously?
Is swelling present at the lateral aspect of the radio-humeral joint?
Do you experience pain (or tenderness) at the lateral aspect of the radiohumeral joint?
-Radiographic confirmation: Check for osteopenia, bone erosions, destruction of the radial head and widening of the sigmoid notch.
-Other laboratories tests as may be necessary
Do you work a manual job?
Have you had a previous history of fracture, dislocation, or gout?
Are extension and flexion movements limited at elbow?
Do you experience increasing limitations in performing self-care (styling hair, dressing, shaving)?
Do you experience pain in the lateral part of the elbow?
Is tenderness present around the joint?
Do you hear a crackling sound on moving the elbow?
Radiographic confirmation – Check for joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation, and subchondral sclerosis.
lumbar: Radiates down one leg		(Rule out sciatica)
Radiates down both legs		(Rule out cauda equina syndrome)
How long have you been experiencing the pain?
Do you experience any numbness or tingling sensation in your legs or feet?
Yes	(Rule out lumbar disc herniation or cauda equina syndrome)
Have you experienced any weakness in your legs?
Have you had any recent trauma or injury to your lower back?
Have you had any previous episodes of low-back pain?
Are you experiencing any bowel or bladder dysfunction, such as difficulty or loss of control in urination or defecation?
Have you had any recent fever, chills, or other signs of infection?
Yes		(Rule out ankylosing spondylitis or bone tumours and infection)
Have you had any recent unintended weight loss?

Lasegue’s Test (Straight-Leg Raise): Examiner raises legs of patient up so that hip flexion is achieved while knee is extended. Test is positive if patient feels pain along the course of the sciatic nerve and below the knee when hip is between 30 - 70 degrees of flexion.
Bragard Test: This is a modification of the SLR and dorsiflexion is applied at the end of the SLR. This reduces the angle where the test is positive.
Do you experience paraesthesia or abnormal sensation in the affected leg?
Does your job or daily tasks involve adopting physically awkward positions?
Do you experience muscle weakness in the affected leg?
Is the pain worsened with flexion of the spine, twisting, bending and/or coughing?
Do you experience pain when elevating the heel or tip-toeing?
Have you had a previous surgical procedure and/or injection recently?
Check if knee jerk reflex is present
Check if ankle jerk reflex is present

Lasegue’s Test (Straight-Leg Raise): Examiner raises legs of patient up so that hip flexion is achieved while knee is extended. Test is positive if patient feels pain along the course of the sciatic nerve and below the knee when hip is between 30 - 70 degrees of flexion.
Bragard Test: This is a modification of the SLR and dorsiflexion is applied at the end of the SLR. This reduces the angle where the test is positive.

Bowstring Test (Popliteal Compression Test/ Tibial Nerve Stretch Sign): This can be done while patient is lying in supine or sitting position. The examiner flexes the knee and applies pressure on the popliteal fossa evoking sciatica.
Is the pain you feel aggravated by prolonged walking, standing and lumbar extension?
Do you experience pain radiating to the buttocks, thigh and legs after walking a certain distance?
Is the numbness you feel over the entire leg?
Is the pain you feel relieved by forward flexion (bending forward)?
Do you find walking upstairs easier than walking downstairs?
Do you experience any form of erectile dysfunction?
Is tenderness present at the lumbar, paraspinal or gluteal region?
Check if a wide-based gait is present
Check if Trendenlenburg’s sign is present
Check if Achilles tendon reflex is diminished

Bicycle Stress Test: Record the amount of time the patient spends on a cycle ergometer while in a neutral lumbar position and slumped position. If patient spends more time in the slumped position, a lumbar spinal stenosis is indicated.

Two-stage Treadmill Test: The patient walks on a flat treadmill and the amount of time spent is recorded and compared with the amount of time while the treadmill is inclined such that patient walks in flexion.

The Lumbar Extension-Loading Test: The examiner ensures that the lumbar region is in moderate extension (10-30 deg) while standing and patient lies supine. Check if there are changes in the symptoms that the patient feels.
Was the pain onset immediately after a heavy lifting?
Does your job or daily task involve long sitting with lateral bending and rotation?
Do your symptoms feel better when lying supine with your hips and knees flexed?
Do you experience pain radiating to buttock and leg?
If yes, does this pain get worse with sitting and relieved with standing?
Check for tenderness in the paraspinal musculature
Check for spasms in the paraspinal musculature
Check for decreased reflexes: patellar and ankle jerk

Lasegue’s Test (Straight-Leg Raise): Examiner raises legs of patient up so that hip flexion is achieved while knee is extended. Test is positive if patient feels pain along the course of the sciatic nerve and below the knee when hip is between 30 - 70 degrees of flexion.
Bragard Test: This is a modification of the SLR and dorsiflexion is applied at the end of the SLR. This reduces the angle where the test is positive.

Bowstring Test (Popliteal Compression Test/ Tibial Nerve Stretch Sign): This can be done while patient is lying in supine or sitting position. The examiner flexes the knee and applies pressure on the popliteal fossa evoking sciatica.
Femoral Nerve Stretch Test (Wasserman sign): Patient is in prone position, knee is flexed and hip is extended. Reproduction of pain in anterior thigh is positive.
Kernig Test: Patient’s neck is flexed, hip flexed and the leg is extended. Pain is reproduced in this position.
Milgram Test: pain is reproduced on straight leg elevation for 30 seconds.
Do you experience pain radiating to both legs?
Do you have a previous history of tumors or trauma to lower back?
Do you experience reduced sensation around your perineal (around genitals) region?
Do you experience reduced sensation in the region of your inner thighs (groin)?
Have you just undergone a lumbar spinal surgery?
Do you find it difficult to initiate urination or experience urinary retention (inability to empty the bladder completely)?
Do you experience bowel incontinence and/or constipation?
Do you experience reduced sexual sensations?
Check for babinski sign and clonus to rule out upper motor neuron involvement
Age (common in teens and early adults)
Do you have a family history of ankylosing spondylitis or any other autoimmune diseases?
Is the pain worse at night and early morning?
Does exercise and physical activity relieve the pain?
Do you experience pain and stiffness in your hips and/or buttocks?
Do exercises and physical activity reduce the associated stiffness?
Do you experience pain and stiffness in your neck or upper back?
Do you experience fatigue, difficulty sleeping and/or general feelings of unwellness?
Do you experience chest pain or discomfort, especially when you take deep breaths?
Have you experienced any symptoms such as red, scaly patches on your skin that may be accompanied by itching or discomfort (psoriasis)?
Have you experienced changes in your posture, such as stooping or hunching forward (question mark sign)?
Have you had any instances of eye inflammation or redness, or increased sensitivity to light (uveitis)?
Lateral lumbar flexion (Finger tip to Floor Test)

The patient stands with heel touching wall and feet wide apart, and back against the wall. Then the examiner measures the distance from middle finger to the floor. Then patient bends to side (lateral flexion). The distance between the middle finger and the floor is measured again. This measurement is done for both sides.

Modified Schrober’s test: Examiner marks both posterior superior iliac spine, and a horizontal line is drawn between both marks. A second line is marked 5cm below the first line, and a third line is marked 10 cm above the first line. Patient is then instructed to flex forward as if attempting to touch their toes.
An increase of less than 5cm, shows decreased lumbar spine range of motion, and ankylosing spondylitis.
Chest expansion- Normal chest expansion is ≥ 5 cm.
Occiput to wall distance- Normally is 0
Tragus to wall distance- Normally is < 15cm
shoulder: What region is pain present in?
Do you have a recent history of fall or trauma in the affected region?
The fracture could be pathological (Ask for blood work-up, calcium level, and Vit D intake)
Do you experience tingling or abnormal sensations?
Do you have swelling in the area of complaint?
Did you notice any unusual movement in the area of complaint?
Do you feel pain when you try to raise your arm?
Is there tenderness around the shoulder region or on palpating root of the neck?
Is your sleep affected by pain?
Is patient adopting a pain avoidance posture by clasping hands together?
Is there a bump or obvious deformity around the clavicle?
Fracture is seen in the middle of the clavicle
Check for how far out of place the broken fragments are (displacement)
What region is pain present in?
Do you experience tingling or abnormal sensations? –
What is the nature of the pain you experience?
If sudden, was it following any of these movements: Abduction ( movement of the arm away from the body sideways), Elevation or External rotation (Rotating shoulder away from the body).
Arm flexion (Movement of the arm forward)
If Pain is felt on Abduction and internal rotation, is pain present in the front or lateral part of the shoulder?
Does your job or daily tasks involve repetitive shoulder movement?
On what movement is the pain felt?
Do you experience weakness on moving your shoulder?
For Subacute tendinitis OR Painfularc syndrome
For how long have you felt this present pain?
Does the pain resolve significantly after the precipitating activity is avoided?
Palpate the anterior edge of the acromion with the arm in extension and flexion. (Subacute Tendinitis). Is pain present?
Have you had a previous history of shoulder pain after repetitive use? (Link to question 11, maybe redundant)
Does pain reduce with rest OR anti-inflammatory use?
Is there tenderness along the bicipital groove or on moving the biceps tendon?
Yes (Biccipital tendinitis can co-exist with other rotator cuff pathologies)
Do you have a previous history of sprain or a jerking movement at the shoulder?
Check if the patient can complete a full abduction of the arm.
If pt, cannot initiate arm ABduction – full tear
If active ABduction is possible – partial tear.

While pt is seated, the examiner applies side flexion of the neck towards the affected side while applying axial compression.
Sign is positive if the pain sensation felt in the shoulder-neck region is replicated.
The painful arc: Ask pt to Abduct arm across range of motion actively
Pain is aggraavated between the range of 60 - 120 deg
Pain is alleviated IF shoulder is laterally rotated on ABduction
Neer Impingement Sign: Stabilise the scapula with one hand and raise the unaffected side to full flexion with Abduction and internal rotation passively.
The test is positive when there is pain at the subacromial space.
Rule out acromioclavicular osteoarthritis, glenohumeral instability and SLAP lesions.
Hawkins-Kennedy Test: Pt arm is placed in 90 deg of elevation at the scapular level and 90 deg flexion; Stabilise the upper arm with one hand and internally rotate with the other hand.
The test is positive when pain is felt in the anterolateral aspect of the shoulder
Jobe’s test: Elevate arm in scapular plane, extend elbow and thumb points towards floor with arm internally rotated. Pt is asked to hold the position with downward pressure from examiner
Positve sign = Pain on examiner restriction indicating supraspinatus irritation.
The ‘Empty Can Test’ for Supraspinatus: Patient is asked to ABduct up to 90 deg, 30 deg flexion and internal rotation. Examiner stands behind patient and applies downward pressure
Positive sign, Affected side is weaker than the unaffected side.
Positive sign suggests tear in the supraspinatus
Infraspinatus Test: Pt holds arms close to body, elbow is flexed up to 90 deg of forward elevation in scapular plane. External rotation is instructed while examiner applies resistance.
Lack of power on one side indicates weakness of the infraspinatus muscle
If test is done with arm in 90 deg elevation
i. Inability to resist external rotation suggests a tear of the infraspinatus
ii. Movement with feelings of pain indicates an irritation to the infraspinatus
Subscapularis (Belly Press Test): Pt is asked to place their hand on their abdomen and move elbow forward. Pt is then asked to press hand on the abdomen.
If subscapularis is torn, Pt will be unable to perform movement and elbow will move backwards.

Lag Sign
For External Rotation: Pt lifts arm slightly from body and is asked to hold in external rotation.
Positive when hand recoils to neutral position and suggests tear of infraspinatus or supraspinatus
Drop Sign. The examiner places the arm at 90 deg ABduction, elbow at Rt angle and arm maximally rotated.
Positive sign, arm drops from position. Suggests a tear of the infraspinatus OR the posterior part of the rotator cuff

The Lift-Off Test (Subscapularis): Pt is asked to place hand on the back at the mid-lumbar spine.
The clinician then lifts hand from the back and pt is asked to hold it there
Positive sign, inability to maintain hand while removed suggests subscapularis weakness.
Did the pain increase drastically within a few hours?
Do you experience pain more at night
Can you perform the movements around the shoulder joint?
Does the pain subside after a few days and the shoulder is restored to normal functioning?
Check X-ray to rule asymptomatic calcification
Speed Test: Place pt’s arm in shoulder flexion, forearm supination and elbow extension. The examiner then applies pressure to the arm as downward action.
A positive sign, pain in the bicipital grove is replicable
Yergasom’s test:  Pt places the forearm in the supine position, and the examiner applies resistance against supination.
A positive sign, this replicates the pain in the bicipital group tenderness.
Ask pt to lift or offer resistance to biceps contraction
Is a prominent lump is seen in lower arm
The upper arm becomes painful and bruised
Hypertrophy and intra-articular entrapment (the hourglass biceps) -(Confirmation)
Ask patient to elevate the shoulder
Do you feel pain on shoulder elevation?
Can the patient elevate the arm up till the end of the range?
Did you have any history of fall or trauma with hand stretched out?
Did you have any history of loading the shoulder region with weight?
Do you feel a ‘painful click’ when you lift your arm above shoulder height?
Do you feel weakness in your arm when doing activities above shoulder height?
Ask the patient to throw to reach a far distance where above shoulder movement is indicated.
Patient is able to (with pain involvement)

Instruct the patient to flex the arm up to 90 deg with the elbow in full extension.
Then ask pt to adduct arm 10-15 deg medial to sagittal plane. Arm is maximally internally rotated and examiner applies downward pressure while patient is asked to resist.  Procedure is then repeated in supination
Positive: Pain is elicited by the first manoeuvre, and is reduced or eliminated by the second.
At the early phase of this pain, did you find it difficult to turn your shoulder out (External rotation) and/or take your arm away from your body (abduction)?
Yes 	(Check for adhesive capsulitis– early phase. Carry out active and passive movements)
Currently, do you find it difficult to turn your shoulder in (Internal rotation) or move your arms towards your body/chest (Adduction)
Yes	(Check for adhesive capsulitis – freezing phase)
Do you find it easier to move your shoulder backwards (extension)?
Do you experience progressive stiffness in your shoulder?
Is the pain worse at night?
Have you had any of the following conditions before: Diabetes, Dupuytren’s, hyperlipidemia, hyperthyroidism, cardiac disease, hemiplegia?
Have you undergone a neurosurgical procedure before?
Previous history of fall or trauma to affected side?
Check if the Active range of motion and passive range of motion is full across shoulder joints
Active and Passive range of motion is limited in Adhesive Capsulitis
Rule out other causes of stiffness with imaging
Have you had a previous history of a shoulder dislocation?
Did the first episode occur with a pull on an abducted, laterally rotated-extended arm?
Do you experience a catching sensation followed by numbness or weakness (Dead-arm syndrome) when making overhead arm movements?
Yes	(Rule out rotator cuff disorder and cervical radiculitis)

Patient is in sitting or lying position, the examiner lifts the arn gently into ABduction, external rotation and then extension. Test is positive if Pt senses that humeral head is about to slip out anteriorly and body stiffens in apprehension.
The test is repeated with the anterior part of shoulder protected.

Pt lies supine, arm ABducted up to 90 deg. The examiner places their hands over the shoulder to set the fulcrum base. The humeral head is levered forward by extending and externally rotating arm. The patient immediately becomes apprehensive.

Patient lies supine , scapula is stabilised with one hand while the upper arm is grasped firmly with the other, and manoeuvring is done forward and backward (like a drawer). The drawer’s test is positive if instability is marked.
Do you have a history of generalised Rheumatoid Arthritis?
Do you experience pain and swelling in both shoulder joints?
Do you have episodes of fever that resolve spontaneously?
Is swelling present anteriorly and superiorly to the shoulder?
Is swelling present in the axilla?
Do you experience increasing limitations in performing self-care (styling hair, dressing, shaving)?
Radiographic confirmation – check for periarticular erosions, subchondral sclerosis, and resorption.
Have you had a previous history of injury, shoulder dislocation, or painful arc syndrome?
Do you feel the pain more at night?
Is your shoulder movement restricted across ALL joint motions?
No	 (Rule out Adhesive Capsulitis. Extension is free in Adhesive Capsulitis)
Do you experience early morning stiffness?
If yes, does the stiffness wane within 30 minutes of waking?
Radiographic confirmation – check for distortion of the joint, subchondral sclerosis, osteophyte formation, and narrowing of articular space.
